speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Read through that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date is July 17, 1995. Survivor is Jack Skovronski. The interviewer is Steven Denenberg. City is Buffalo Grove, Illinois, in the United States of America. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewer
text: July 17, 1995. The survivor's name is Jack Skovronski. My name is Steve Denenberg. We're recording the interview in Buffalo Grove, Illinois, in the United States of America. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anytime. Quiet please.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, I'd like to start by asking you to state your name and spell-- spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jack Skovronski, S-K-O-V-R-O-N-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my name at birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birth date is July 13, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 75. I was-- just turned 75 four days ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a little town called Przedecz in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Small little town--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me just ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell-- spell Przedecz?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-R-Z-E-D-E-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right, tell me-- tell me something about the town. What kind of town was Przedecz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little town with a-- not too big of a population of Jewish-- Jewish population. And 1939-- shall I start like--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, tell me about what was the life like before-- before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- my father was a businessman. He was dealing with the grain, and anything that he-- it produces. And of course, I had two older brothers, which were in the business. And I was, at that time, 19. So I started to help out my-- in the business, just like my older brothers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Socially, we had a couple of Jewish organizations. I belonged to one of them. And you want me to mention the name? Yeah, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- It was the Betar. That was a Revisionist organization. And their vision is group of the Zionist movement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did expect the war coming, but there was no way out. Nobody could run from the city at will. I mean, you can go from city to city, but not out of the country. So we waited and see what is going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: September 1, the war broke out. September 1, 1939, the war broke out and-- you want me to continue? Well, let me ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a little more detail about your life. Tell me-- Tell me, who were the members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had three brothers and two sisters. One sister was married. One was just about three years older than I. She went to school out of town. And my older brothers were active in Zionist movements. They were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one brother was 10 years older than I, and the other one was, like, 16 years older than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One name was Chaim, Henry; and Azriel. The older one was Azriel. Sigmund, in Polish. And they were very active in our father's business. And just before the war, a year, my younger brother got married and moved out of the city to the city where he married-- married the girl. So I became more active in the business, started to. Any other--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- what were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. My parents name was-- my father's name was Natan. My mother's name was Touga. And my sister's name was Rivka and Hella.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your family always lived in this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, yeah. All the time in that town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how many people lived in Przedecz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 500 Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the total-- what was the-- how many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Total, I wouldn't exactly know. But I would say two-thirds more of Polish people than-- than Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relation between the Polish people and the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- in-- before the war, it wasn't too bad. But when the war started, they got carried away by the Germans, and they're trying to help the Germans as much as they could to show who is a Jew and who is not a Jew. And they were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not too big of a help to the Jewish people. Of course, they couldn't do much, but if there was something to be done, it wasn't done anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any anti-Semitic events, say, as you were growing up, when you were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had small incidents, like the guy that rented the-- the-- the place where we were organized, the Betar, when he got drunk, [LAUGHS] he said he's going to kill all the Jews there and that-- and he was the landlord of our-- but when he got sober, he calmed down and he apologized for what he did. And-- and-- and that was the life. I mean, that was the thing that happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1939, of course, when the war broke out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans came in approximately about 10 days after the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's wait a little bit before we talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little more about the town. Where was it located in-- in relation to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the relations to-- the town is called Przedecz. And it's the [POLISH] in [POLISH]. That means the-- the-- the state was like [POLISH]. And that was, at one time, German territory before the first World War. So when they came in to-- they annexed our part of Poland to Germany, and the city was called Moosburg in German. It comes to me back while I'm talking. And I was still at home, and they started to-- the Germans started to do things against the Jews. Anything that-- we were forced labor. And I used to go to a place where they made alcohol-- the alcohol from potatoes and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the beets, sugar beets. And we were working there for a while. And we came home at night to our homes, and we saw that the situation is not-- not rosy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Let me go-- I want to go back a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before we talk about the war. Before the war started, did-- were you-- were you aware of the politics going on in Germany or outside of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we read papers. We had a paper, and we knew what's going on in the world. But we couldn't go no place. There would-- all the-- all the doors were closed to immigrants. A few families from our town went to Brazil, one family. Another family went-- because they had family there before. And-- but not many-- very many people left town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned your father was a businessman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had mentioned, when we talked earlier, he was involved in a-- like a bank.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a bank that actually with the headquarters in Warsaw. It's a strictly Jewish bank that helped people, like, producing a-- older-- like, they were tailors or shoemakers or-- or-- or-- so they can manufacture the product-- the product for a whole week, because they didn't have enough money there on their own. And there was strictly a non-profitable organization. No interest was charged for the money. And after they-- the custom in-- in those days were that every day in a-- in a neighboring city was a market. That's where they went to sell their product. So after they came-- after they sold the product and made a living-- a poor living, but living that they could support their family-- and they paid back the money so other people can benefit from-- from that bank. My father was the treasurer of the bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: And active in Jewish life in the little city. Was it-- what was the Jewish life like there? What-- how were holidays celebrated? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- you see, in the old country, everybody was religious. There were no factions of lesser religion. Everybody-- either you were religious or you-- or you were not at all. So practically everybody was religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to shul on Saturday, every Saturday morning. And I went with my father, and we celebrated all the holidays. And just like-- we're not assimilated. We're more traditional life. Were you-- were you bar mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I had bar mitzvah at 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not as-- as extravagant like they do in this country, but there was a kiddish, which is the father invited the closest friends. And they had cake, and-- and had drank and-- and did just about all. No-- no meal was served, of a kiddish. And it's cake and-- and drinks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of-- were there other relatives who lived in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my father had a sister, married, in our-- with a husband and one girl. And my mother just came from the next city to us, which is about 15 kilometers from our town. So on Pesach, [YIDDISH], Pesach is two days, and then is a free time so we can drive, we can write. Of course, no-- no cars at that time, so we had horses and buggies, and we went to visit our grandpa, grandfather, which lived in the other city. It's called Izbica. And-- and that was probably once a year. And once a year our grandpa came to us for a holiday to-- to visit. I really don't remember exactly how he perished, my grandfather. I just don't remember. Many times I lay down and-- and I can't recall exactly what happened to my grandpa. Of course, my parents, I know, perished and the concentration camps called Chelmno. They evacuated them-- all-- the whole city-- whoever was left-- to Chelmno, and there was Judenrein, 'clean of Jews.' No more in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war started, what did you-- what did you expect the future held for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we thought that someday we're going to go to Palestine and build a new life, because we didn't see a big future in Poland. We didn't see too big of a future. There were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no-- no possibilities to explore your-- your abilities or whatever. But we thought that some day that England will open the doors and let in more Jews to Palestine, so we can come and build a new life. But I was 19 at that time, so we didn't-- there was no-- no-- no way of getting out. And there was a couple of guys that went to a Hachshara, which is a-- a movement, because a certain amount of certificates the British government allowed to immigrate. So a couple of guys from our town went to Israel, and one got killed in the war-- in the war of liberation in Israel. And the other one's still around.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now let's talk about September 1, 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, September 1st, the war broke out. And we knew that there is no good thing to expect. So the Germans came in after eight, 10 days, and they started to intimidate the Jews and do all things that was never to heard of. They were taking a couple of guys in the street and putting on a sign on their chest that I am a black marketeer, and-- which was absolutely not true. And-- and it-- it was tragic common moment-- moments that we saw no good future. But nothing you could do. You are in that. One day, being 19, I came to my father and said, Dad, not much I can help you here anymore. Your business is going to be taken away. I will only be a burden to you. I want to go away. They're talking about the other half of Poland, the Russians occupied, and that might be a easier life to live. Easier not being afraid of being a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day we organized about four, five people. And we decided we're going to go. So we went to Warsaw, and we heard that somebody is-- is organizing a group to cross the Bug, the River Bug, which was on the other side of Russia-- the other side of Poland, in the Russian part of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came there. It was the end of January. It was cold. It was winter. But it was-- I came to a town called Rowne, and I met a lady, which was married to a man from that town. They lived there, and I moved in with them and stayed with them for a short while. And looking for a job, oh, any kind of a job. There were easy dollar-- I mean, easy ruble to make. At that time, it was rubles. You know, Russian currency.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- cleaning shoes for the Russian officers on the street. But there-- there weren't too many jobs available for-- especially for the refugees which came from the other part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was alone, of course. No big help from a lot of people. I had to make my own decisions, what to do with my life. After being there for, like, four months, five months, the Russians wanted all the people, all the refugees, that was like a-- they were calling that the borderline, because I don't know if they expected the Germans to attack Russia at that time, but that was 1940. And the-- the Germans attacked Russia in 1941. But by that time, I was already in Siberia. They came one morning to the door and knocked on the door. They knew-- I don't know how they know, but they knew that somebody lives here which is a refugee. So they knocked on the door, and they asked me my name. And I stated my name. And I-- he says, come to the office of the NKVD. It's the secret police. And we'll question you, and we'll let you go. I said, let me take a few things with me. He says, no, you don't have to. You'll be, after three, four hours, released. That was not true. When they arrested us, they put us in trains, cattle trains, and sent us to Siberia. That was June, 1940. And they sent us to Siberia, and we started to work there. I was young, and I did work very hard in order to-- because the Russian system says if you work, they call it [RUSSIAN], a-- something that you specialize in more and-- and better work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we were a group of 10. We were cutting trees in the woods. So we worked very hard, and the-- and there was a [RUSSIAN] that they gave. A-- you have to do so much. By 12 o'clock, we're through doing so much, so whatever we did from 12:00 to 3:00 or to 4:00, because-- so we got the extra piece of bread, extra cigarette. And we're treated a little better than the general population.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Russian families, too, in-- in the camp. For some reason, that they send them there for-- that I-- I really don't know why. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we worked hard, and we stayed there without knowing when the end will come. I mean, what-- what is the future bringing? So we worked there. We were there 14 months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard that the General Sikorski, a Polish general, being in London forming a Polish government in London. And they'll take all the refugees all to the-- to form a Polish army and get out of the country, out of Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was very much interested in that. So they released us from Siberia, and they said, you can go anyplace you want on your own. So we went to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Asia-- Russian Asia, like Uzbekistan, Turkestan, Tashkent.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went in these parts of Russia. The situation-- economical station was there worse than in Siberia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were so many Russian refugees. I forgot to mention that in the meantime, Germany attacked Russia in 1940-- I don't exactly remember the date. But the Germans were marching in with a speed till-- not far from Moscow. I don't exactly know, but they were-- they were saying they're very close to Moscow. But then the Russians formed in the [INAUDIBLE] the-- the conditions in Russia, the-- the nature conditions in Russia were so good for the Russian people, that the German army froze to death. They were freezing. The soldiers-- the German soldiers were freezing. And that's when they had that fight under Stalingrad, which is in between Russia and-- in between Kiev and Moscow on the-- the Stalingrad. And they defended their country, and they did a good job on the Germans, because as far as we know, in 1944, they were advancing to Poland and coming into Germany, the Russian Army. In the meantime, they were forming a Polish army in a place called Buzuluk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I wanted desperately to get there so I can get out of-- they were saying that the Polish army is going out. So I was desperately wanted to get out of the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there to Buzuluk, but they didn't accept Jewish people in the Polish army. They were saying this. But I had a friend of mine, his brother was a doctor. And they were in desperate need of doctors, because the sicknesses were unbearable in Russia. All kind of dysentery and typhus and all kind of sicknesses. So the-- he had a brother. His brother got a job in the office, being a Jewish man. So I walked in in the office. There was a Polish officer, and I said, I would like to join the Polish army. So he said, what's your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said my name is Jacob Skovronski. And the other guy that I mentioned was sitting there. He says, as far as I know, there were no Jewish names on the-- Jewish people under that name.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got-- he tore off of a cigarette butt piece of paper and gave me a permit that I can go to-- to join the families of the Polish families that joined the army. They-- they sent us to to a port city on the Caspian Sea.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called Krasnovodsk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got there, and being that the Russians didn't want no-- too many people there, so whoever was in that city, they pressed everybody to get on that boat to go overseas. And I was among them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just stop right now and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.
